From: Wrapper[Alpha,Byte]~, Wrapper[Alpha,Byte]~, Wrapper[Alpha,Byte]~, DataType[Alpha,Alpha]~, DataType[Alpha,Alpha]~, DataType[Alpha,Alpha]~, Extensible[Alpha]~, Extensible[Alpha]~, Extensible[Alpha]~.

Angle[Self:imm]

The super type of Radians and Degrees, used to enforce common behaviour

Angles that normalize to the same value are considered equal,

`Degrees#(+360.0)! == Degrees#(+0.0)! .assertTrue`

`Radians#(Math.tau / -4.0)! == Degrees#(+3.0 / +4.0 * Math.tau)! .assertTrue`

Byte

Byte: 8-bit unsigned [0,255]. Ordering is UNSIGNED.

Note: / is not provided; use div/rem (divisor is Nat).

Extends: Sealed, DataType[Byte,Byte], SumNumber[Byte], Extensible[Byte], OrderHash[Byte], Order[Byte], ToImm[Byte], ToInfo, ToStr, WidenTo[Byte]

Methods

read !=(read Byte):Bool

From: DataType[Byte,Byte]!=, OrderHash[Byte]!=, Order[Byte]!=.

**(Byte):Byte

checked power; throws on overflow

*(Byte):Byte

checked mul; throws on overflow

+(Byte):Byte

checked add; throws on overflow (past 255)

-(Byte):Byte

checked sub; throws on underflow (below 0)

.aluAddWrap(Byte):Byte

u8 wraparound add (mod 256)

.aluAnd(Byte):Byte

u8 and

.aluDiv(Byte):Byte

u8 trunc division; throws iff x == 0

.aluMulWrap(Byte):Byte

u8 wraparound mul (mod 256)

.aluOr(Byte):Byte

u8 or

.aluRem(Byte):Byte

paired with aluDiv; throws iff x == 0

.aluShiftLeft(Nat):Byte

u8 shift-left; effective shift = bits & 7 (i.e., modulo 8).

.aluShiftRight(Nat):Byte

u8 logical shift-right; effective shift = bits & 7.

.aluSubWrap(Byte):Byte

u8 wraparound sub (mod 256)

.aluXor(Byte):Byte

u8 xor

~~(Byte):mut Flow[Byte]

Range from this to Nat-1 included. If end <= this, an empty range is produced.

~~=(Byte):mut Flow[Byte]

Range from this to Nat included, If end < this, an empty range is produced. If end == this, a singleton range is produced.

DataTypeBy[E:imm,K:imm,K0:imm]

DataTypeBy is the "by key" conversion, analogous to OrderHashBy.

It returns a closed DataType[K,K0] for each source value t: T.

Examples:

- If Key implements DataType[Key,Key0], then {::.key} : DataTypeBy[Foo,Key,Key0].

- {::} is the identity-by when T itself implements DataType[T,T0] (see DataTypeBy[T,T0] below).

Extends: ToInfoBy[E], ToImmBy[E,K0], OrderHashBy[E,K], OrderBy[E,K], ToStrBy[E]

Methods

#(read E):read DataType[K,K0]

No documentation yet.

From: ToInfoBy[E]#, ToImmBy[E,K0]#, OrderHashBy[E,K]#, OrderBy[E,K]#, ToStrBy[E]#.

.hideKey:OrderHashBy[E]

From: OrderHashBy[E,K].hideKey.

.then[_AAK0:imm](OrderBy[E,_AAK0]):OrderBy[E]

From: OrderHashBy[E,K].then, OrderBy[E,K].then.

.thenData[K1:imm,K10:imm,E0:imm](DataTypeBy[E,K1,K10],F[K0,K10,E0]):DataTypeBy[E,E0]

Lexicographic composition on keys; you choose how to merge imm reps.

Str merge is by ", " concat; Info merge uses Info+

.thenDataLeft[K1:imm,K10:imm](DataTypeBy[E,K1,K10]):DataTypeBy[E,K0]

Convenience: keep left imm rep (often fine for "by key then tie-breaker").

.thenHash[_AK0:imm](OrderHashBy[E,_AK0]):OrderHashBy[E]

From: OrderHashBy[E,K].thenHash.

.view[_AAA:imm](F[read _AAA,read E]):OrderBy[_AAA]

From: OrderHashBy[E,K].view, OrderBy[E,K].view.

.viewData[A:imm](F[read A,read E]):DataTypeBy[A,K,K0]

No documentation yet.

.viewHash[_AA:imm](F[read _AA,read E]):OrderHashBy[_AA]

From: OrderHashBy[E,K].viewHash.

DataTypeBy[T:imm,T0:imm]

Specialized "datatype for T" (no external key type).

This mirrors OrderHashBy[T]: you provide per-value ops, and # closes into a DataType[T,T0].

Extends: DataTypeBy[T,T,T0], OrderBy[T,T], OrderHashBy[T,T], ToImmBy[T,T0], ToInfoBy[T], ToStrBy[T]

DataType[T:imm,T0:imm,E:*,E0:imm]

Error

Exception classes (observability):

- deterministic: The same operation called with observationally identical input

will either always throw or never throw.

Catchable anywhere via Try#.

- nonDeterministic: the throw may or may not happen in the same abstract situation

(OOM, stack overflow, asserts, etc).

Catching/observing requires mut CapTry obtained from mut System.

Asserts are nonDeterministic because another standard library may legally replace

them with no-ops.

#[R:**](R,F[R,Bool],F[R]):R

No documentation yet.

Float

Float: 64 bits IEEE values. Note: ==/order instead follow the usual rules:

- NaN == NaN (all different NaNs equal); NaN is greater than everything else including +Inf

- -0.0 == +0.0

Method hash must respect this ordering

Extends: Sealed, DataType[Float,Float], SumNumber[Float], _AssertApprox[Float], DataType[Float,Float], Extensible[Float], OrderHash[Float], Order[Float], ToImm[Float], ToInfo, ToStr, WidenTo[Float]

.byte:Byte

clamp+trunc0; NaN->0; +Inf->maxByte; -Inf->0 (never throws)

.byteExact:Opt[Byte]

.some if finite, integral, in [0,maxByte]; .empty otherwise

.ceil:Int

ceil(this) (policy: clamp+trunc0 into Int)

.clamp(Float,Float):Float

clamp into [lo,hi]; throws if lo>hi

.ieeeEq(Float):Bool

IEEE == (NaN != NaN), but still +0.0 == -0.0

.ieeeRemainder(Float):Float

IEEE remainder r = x - n*d with n nearest integer to x/d (ties-to-even)

.ieeeSameBits(Float):Bool

raw bits compare: it distinguishes NaN payloads and signed zero

.ieeeStr:Str

IEEE 754 round-trip decimal string for this Float (includes NaN/Inf)

.int:Int

clamp+trunc0; NaN->0; +Inf->maxInt; -Inf->minInt (never throws)

.intExact:Opt[Int]

.some if finite, integral, in [minInt,maxInt]; .empty otherwise

.isInfinite:Bool

true iff +/-Inf

.isNaN:Bool

true iff NaN (any payload)

.isNegInfinity:Bool

true iff -Inf

.isNegZero:Bool

true iff raw bits are -0.0

.isPosInfinity:Bool

true iff +Inf

.isPosZero:Bool

true iff raw bits are +0.0

.nat:Nat

clamp+trunc0; NaN->0; +Inf->maxNat; -Inf->0 (never throws)

.natExact:Opt[Nat]

.some if finite, integral, in [0,maxNat]; .empty otherwise

.numExact:Opt[Num]

.some if finite (and convertible under chosen policy); .empty on NaN/Inf

.round:Int

clamp into Int range, round-to-nearest integer, ties-to-even; NaN->0; +Inf->maxInt; -Inf->minInt (never throws)

.signnum:Float

Return the sign of this number: +/-0.0 if x is +/-0.0, +1.0 if x is positive, -1.0 if x is negative, NaN if x.

.sqrt:Float

IEEE sqrt; returns NaN iff this is negative (excluding -0)

From: F[Tests,Tests]#.

FloatMatch[R:**]

A matcher for floats, has all the special cases of float:

1. Regular floats (which are none of the following)

2. +0.0 and -0.0 (numbers can get rounded/FPE'd to these)

3. +inf and -inf

4. NaN

mut .regular(Float):R

No documentation yet.

FloatMatches[R:**]

A Factory for constructing a FloatMatch, provides some constructors

If you need one more custom than this method provides,

it is recommended to just build a FloatMatch by hand

Methods

#(mut MF[Float,R],mut MF[R]):mut FloatMatch[R]

Supply nan# if the float f is NaN, otherwise number#(f)

read #(Tests):Tests

From: F[Tests,Tests]#.

InMemoryLog[T:imm]

Logs values in memory.

Use by defining a capture-free singleton log:

```

PointLog: InMemoryLog[Point]{"PointLog"}

...//usage

PointLog.log(myPoint)

..//later, reading the log and confirming is what we expect

PointLog.reader(sys).consume.assertEq({::},Lists#(myPoint))

```

In memory logs are particularly useful for unit testing of immutable computation via mock objects.

The name is only diagnostic, to be used for debugging, test reports, and failure messages.

Two different logs with the same name do not interfere with each other.

The log is kept in runtime memory. Calling .log appends a value to the

current in-memory contents of this log.

Calling .reader creates a reader for this log through System.

The reader can inspect or consume the values currently stored for this log.

Concrete log types must be non generic.

Creating a reader throw with nondeterministic error if the concrete log type is generic.

Extends: CaptureFree

Methods

.log(T):Void

Submits s to an in memory log that requires a capability to be read.

.name:Str

No documentation yet.

.reader(mut System):mut LogReader[T]

Convenience over calling `sys.readLog(this)`

Int

Int: signed 64-bit.

Note: / is not provided; use div/rem (divisor is Nat).

Extends: Sealed, DataType[Int,Int], SumNumber[Int], Extensible[Int], OrderHash[Int], Order[Int], ToImm[Int], ToInfo, ToStr, WidenTo[Int]

Methods

read !=(read Int):Bool

From: DataType[Int,Int]!=, OrderHash[Int]!=, Order[Int]!=.

**(Nat):Int

checked power; throws on overflow/underflow. Uses Nat to make negative powers impossible as they would produce fractional results

*(Int):Int

checked mul; throws on overflow/underflow

+(Int):Int

checked add; throws on overflow/underflow

-(Int):Int

checked sub; throws on overflow/underflow

.abs:Nat

abs; x if x >= 0, -x otherwise. Returns a Nat sice Math.minInt.abs is larger than Math.maxInt, so it would be unrepresentable as an Int.

.aluAddWrap(Int):Int

i32 wraparound add (mod 2^32)

.aluAnd(Int):Int

i32 and

.aluByte:Byte

low 8 bits (wrap), not clamp

.aluDiv(Int):Int

i32 trunc division; throws iff x == 0; Note: negative divisor allowed here

.aluMulWrap(Int):Int

i32 wraparound mul (mod 2^32)

.aluNat:Nat

reinterpret i32 bits as u32 Nat (bit-cast)

.aluOr(Int):Int

i32 or

.aluRem(Int):Int

paired with aluDiv; sign follows dividend; throws iff x == 0

.aluShiftLeft(Nat):Int

i32 shift-left; effective shift = bits & 31.

.aluShiftRight(Nat):Int

i32 arithmetic shift-right (sign-ext); effective shift = bits & 31.

.aluSubWrap(Int):Int

i32 wraparound sub (mod 2^32)

.aluXor(Int):Int

i32 xor

.num:Num

converts to Num (note: Num can represent exactly all Int values)

.pred:Int

predecessor number; throws on underflow

.rem(Nat):Int

r = this - (this.div(d))*d; throws iff d==0;

.sign:Int

Return the sign of this number: +0 if x is 0, +1 if x is positive, -1 if x is negative.

.sqrt:Float

approximate sqrt; returns NaN iff this is negative

.wrapIndex(Nat):Nat

normalize into [0,len-1]; throws iff len==0; example: -1.wrapIndex(7) == 6

/(Nat):Num

create a Num represening the exact result of this division; throws iff d==0

List[E:*]

List[E] is the standard persistent sequence.

It also supports DataType[List[E],List[imm E],E,imm E] operations:

.imm/.str/.info/.cmp/.hash/.close (see DataType docs).

Extends: _List[E], _AssertEmpty, _AssertSize, DataType[List[E],List[imm E],E,imm E], Extensible[List[E]], OrderHash[List[E],E], Order[List[E],E], Sealed, ToImm[E,imm E,List[imm E]], ToInfo[E], ToStr[E], WidenTo[List[E]]

read #(Tests):Tests

No documentation yet.

From: F[Tests,Tests]#.

Log

Log strings in a text file.

The log is written to .out/logs/_base/log.log.

Each call appends the string representation of s as one timestamped line.

The timestamp is in UTC and is added by the runtime.

The log is circular across runs:

- when logging starts, an existing non-empty live log is moved to a

stamped archive file;

- when the program shuts down, the current non-empty live log is also

moved to a stamped archive file;

- empty live logs are deleted;

- only a bounded number of archived logs is kept. (currently 10, this may change with future versions)

If a previous live log is found when logging starts, the archived copy is

marked as interrupted. This usually means the previous process did not

complete normal shutdown.

Logging failures are reported to the runtime error log when possible.

Only a bounded number of error logs is kept. (currently 100, this may change with future versions)

read #(Tests):Tests

From: F[Tests,Tests]#.

Map[K:imm,E:*]

Keys are only imm and compared/hashed using the map's stored OrderHashBy[K,...].

The flow order, .str order etc all respect/follow the map insertion order.

Extends: Sealed, DataType[Map[K,E],Map[K,imm E],E,imm E], _AssertEmpty, _AssertSize, Extensible[Map[K,E]], OrderHash[Map[K,E],E], Order[Map[K,E],E], ToImm[E,imm E,Map[K,imm E]], ToInfo[E], ToStr[E], WidenTo[Map[K,E]]

Methods

mut ++(mut Map[K,E]):mut Map[K,E]

union; Note: the left map has priority: elements in left are not overridden by elements in right; and the key Order is always the left one (left map's ordering reinterprets right map's keys)

read ++(read Map[K,E]):read Map[K,E]

union; Note: the left map has priority: elements in left are not overridden by elements in right; and the key Order is always the left one (left map's ordering reinterprets right map's keys)

map elements to an imm map with same keys

read .matcher:iso FloatMatch[Nat]

No documentation yet.

Math

Question: How many methods should be added here?

Stuff like .abs and .sqrt are defined on the numeric types themselves, so they are not included here, should they be?

What about .sin, .cos, .tan, .log, .exp, etc? Should they be included here?

What about other constants like Pi and E? When do they get too obscure to be worth including here?

Extends: Sealed

Methods

.acos(Float):Radian

Returns the arc sine of a value; the returned angle is in [0, pi].

throws if the argument is NaN or its absolute value is greater than 1.

.asin(Float):Radian

Returns the arc sine of a value; the returned angle is in [-pi/2, pi/2].

Throws if the argument is NaN or its absolute value is greater than 1, NaN.

If the argument is zero, then the result is a zero with the same sign as the argument.

.atan(Float):Radian

Returns the arc tangent of a value; the returned angle is in [-pi/2, pi/2].

throws if the argument is NaN.

If the argument is zero, then the result is a zero with the same sign as the argument.

.atan2(Float,Float):Radian

Returns the angle theta from the conversion of rectangular coordinates (x, y) to polar coordinates (r, theta).

This method computes the phase theta by computing an arc tangent of y / x in [-pi to pi]

Throws if either x or y is NaN.

.byteBits:Nat

The number of bits in the binary representation of a Byte (8)

.e:Float

The mathematical constant e

.euclideanMod(Float,Float):Float

Return the euclidean modulus of two floats.

This is the "non-negative" mod if the divisor is positive:

Math.euclideanMod(-3.0, +7.0) .assertEq +4.0;

Math.euclideanMod(+3.0, +7.0) .assertEq +3.0;

.floatBits:Nat

The number of bits in the binary representation of a Float (64)

.hypot(Float,Float):Float

Returns (x** +2.0 + y** +2.0).sqrt without intermediate overflow or underflow.

Throws if either argument is NaN

.inf:Float

The representation of positive infinity in the Float type

.intBits:Nat

The number of bits in the binary representation of an Int (64)

.ln(Float):Float

The natural log of the given float, returns:

throws if x NaN or < 0,

returns negative infinity if x = 0,

returns positive infinity if x is positive infinity

.log(Float):Float

The log base 10 of the given float, returns:

throws if x NaN or < 0,

-ve infinity if x = 0,

+ve infinity if x is positive infinity,

log base 10 of x otherwise;

.log2(Float):Float

Returns:

throws if x NaN or < 0, or b <= 0,

-ve infinity if x = 0,

+ve infinity if x is positive infinity,

log base 2 of x otherwise;

.logb(Float,Float):Float

The log base `base` of the given float, returns:

throws if base, or x is NaN or < 0, or b <= 0,

-ve infinity if x = 0,

+ve infinity if x is positive infinity,

log base 10 of x otherwise;

.match[R:*](Float,mut FloatMatch[R]):R

Match over all the special variants of float weirdness.

Allows to match over +/-0.0, NaN, +/-inf, and pass a custom function for

the other regular numbers. Recommended to use with `FloatMatches`.

.max[T:*,K:imm](T,T,OrderBy[imm T,K]):T

Return the maximum of two elements given the ordering.

Will return the first one if the elements are equal.

.maxByte:Byte

The largest representable Byte (255)

.maxInt:Int

The largest representable Int (2^63 - 1)

.maxNat:Nat

The largest representable Nat (2^64 - 1)

.min[T:imm,K:imm](T,T,OrderBy[T,K]):T

Return the minimum of two elements given the ordering.

Will return the first one if the elements are equal.

.minByte:Byte

The smallest representable Byte (0)

.minInt:Int

The smallest representable Int (-2^63)

.minNat:Nat

The smallest representable Nat (0)

.nan:Float

The representation of NaN in the Float type

.natBits:Nat

The number of bits in the binary representation of a Nat (64)

.negInf:Float

The representation of negative infinity in the Float type

.pi:Float

The mathematical constant Pi

.tau:Float

The mathematical constant Tau (2 * Pi)

Methods

read !=(read Nat):Bool

From: DataType[Nat,Nat]!=, OrderHash[Nat]!=, Order[Nat]!=.

**(Nat):Nat

checked power; throws on overflow.

*(Nat):Nat

checked mul; throws on overflow

+(Nat):Nat

checked add; throws on overflow (past maxNat)

-(Nat):Nat

checked sub; throws on underflow (below 0)

.aluAddWrap(Nat):Nat

u32 wraparound add (mod 2^32)

.aluAnd(Nat):Nat

u32 and

.aluByte:Byte

low 8 bits (wrap), not clamp

.aluDiv(Nat):Nat

u32 trunc division; throws iff x == 0

.aluInt:Int

reinterpret u32 bits as i32 Int (bit-cast)

.aluMulWrap(Nat):Nat

u32 wraparound mul (mod 2^32)

.aluOr(Nat):Nat

u32 or

.aluRem(Nat):Nat

paired with aluDiv; throws iff x == 0

.aluShiftLeft(Nat):Nat

u32 shift-left; effective shift = bits & 31 (i.e., modulo 32).

.aluShiftRight(Nat):Nat

u32 logical shift-right; effective shift = bits & 31.

.aluSubWrap(Nat):Nat

u32 wraparound sub (mod 2^32)

.aluXor(Nat):Nat

u32 xor

.round:Num

round-to-nearest integer, ties-to-even (exact on rationals); result is an integer (denominator == 1)

.sign:Num

Return the sign of this number: +0/1 if x is 0, +1/1 if x is positive, -1/1 if x is negative.

.sqrt:Float

Extends: F[Str,Str,Str,Nat,StackFrame]

Methods

read #(Str,Str,Str,Nat):StackFrame

No documentation yet.

From: F[Str,Str,Str,Nat,StackFrame]#.

Str

- canonicalization: `+00_12`.intExact.str ==`+12`

Str.natExact:

- rejected: `+12`.natExact `-1`.natExact `1_`.natExact `_1`.natExact `4_294_967_296`.natExact (range)

- accepted: `0`.natExact `00`.natExact `0__10`.natExact `4_294_967_295`.natExact

- canonicalization: `000_255`.nat.str ==`255` and `4_294_967_295`.nat.str==`4294967295`

Str.byteExact:

- rejected: `+12`.byteExact `-1`.byteExact `1_`.byteExact `_1`.byteExact `256`.byteExact

- accepted: `0`.byteExact `00`.byteExact `0__10`.byteExact `255`.byteExact `000_255`.byteExact

- canonicalization: `000_255`.byteExact.str==`255`

Str.numExact:

- rejected: `12`.numExact `+12`.numExact `+1/`.numExact `+1./2`.numExact `+1/_2`.numExact `+1/0`.numExact `+1.0/0.0`.numExact

- accepted: `+12/1`.numExact `-00_12/000_3`.numExact `+12.4/3.2`.numExact `+0/1`.numExact `-0/1`.numExact

- canonicalization: `+12.4/3.2`.numExact!.str==`+31/8` `+12/2`.numExact!.str==`+6/1`

Str.floatExact (IEEE 754 binary 64):

- rejected (syntax): `1.0`.floatExact `+1`.floatExact `+1.`.floatExact `+.1`.floatExact `+1e0`.floatExact

- rejected (value): `+NaN`.floatExact `+Infinity`.floatExact `-Infinity`.floatExact `+0.1`.floatExact

- Float value 0.1 (and many others) do not exist; so it is rejected.

- accepted: `+0.5`.floatExact `+1.25`.floatExact `+01__0.00__1`.floatExact `+1.0e-3`.floatExact

- canonicalization: `-0.0`.floatExact.str==`+0.0`

Str.ieeeFloat:

- rejected: `duck`.ieeeFloat

- accepted: `NaN`.ieeeFloat `Infinity`.ieeeFloat `-Infinity`.ieeeFloat `-0.0`.ieeeFloat `+0.0`.ieeeFloat `1e0`.ieeeFloat

- canonicalization: `1e0`.ieeeFloat!.ieeeStr==`1.0` and `+0.0`.ieeeFloat!.ieeeStr==`0.0` (NaN payload not preserved)

- roundtrip (value): f.ieeeStr.ieeeFloat! == f

The roundtrip also preserves the sign of zero but not the NaN payload

`0.1`.ieeeFloat!.ieeeStr == `0.1` BUT there is no Float representing 0.1

.ieeeFloat + .ieeeStr implement shortest string that round-trips under round-to-nearest ties-to-even

That is, `0.1`.ieeeFloat!.ieeeStr lies to you twice, once when it creates the float, and a secon time when

it choses to print a near value that has a compact representation instead of the actual content of the float

Another example: `0.100000000000000005`.ieeeFloat!.ieeeStr == `0.1`

But, `0.100000000000000005` is closer to the actual representable Float value. That is,

`+0.1`.ieeeFloat!.numExact!.inRange(+0.100000000000000005/1, +2/1) holds

`+0.1`.ieeeFloat!.numExact!.inRange(+0.1/1, +0.100000000000000005/1) does not hold

.escape:Str

returns a string representing valid Fearless code which yields this string when evaluated (may use ^ and |, not necessarily a single literal)

.floatExact:Opt[Float]

parse a string representing a valid Fearless Float; .empty on invalid (this includes NaN and +/-Inf since not syntactically valid Float, and values that would round)

read .hash:Nat

From: DataType[Str,Str].hash, OrderHash[Str].hash.

.ieeeFloat:Opt[Float]

parse IEEE-754 string; accepts Float.ieeeStr output; .empty on invalid (returns the rounded Float value (ties-to-even), preserving NaN/Inf and signed zero. Float.ieeeStr.ieeeFloat produces the same Float modulo NaN payload; but Str.ieeeFloat.ieeeStr may not produce the same string due to canonicalization+rounding)

ToImm[T0:imm]

DataType sits on top of OrderHash:

Order > OrderHash > DataType

It adds:

- Info (for easy serialization/deserialization/exceptions)

- deep imm conversion

- widen/equality helpers (defined elsewhere)

Fixpoint story:

- OrderHash[T] already provides the method .close: T allowing to see this as a T

- DataType adds the opposite direction .close(t: T): DataType[...] allowing to this T as DataType[T]

Basically, by implementing those two methods we close the type fixpoint making T and the DataType the same logical type.

Methods

deep immutable representation (may change type)

ToStr

Ordering + hashing via "closed fixpoints".

Intuition:

- An Order[T] is a comparator that is also a *closed* value: .close: T.

- Most concrete T implement Order[T] with .close->this.

- OrderBy[T,K] is a projection: given t: T, build a closed Order[K] for its key.

- OrderHash adds hashing and ToStr. This is all the Maps/Set may need.

Methods

read .str:Str

string representation

ToStrBy[T:imm]

per-value printer: given x: T, produce a ToStr for x

Try

Strong exception safety is upheld:

The operation has either completed successfully or thrown an exception,

leaving the program state exactly as it was before the operation started.

UStr

Unicode string type

This string type is designed to support the evil concept of Unicode strings.

UStr is an intentionally minimal type representing a sequence of Nats in the ranges

0..55295 and 57344..1114111.

Yes, there is a hole in the allowed values, as required by the Unicode standard for 'scalar values'.

Those numbers are conventionally written in hexadecimal (base 16) using digits 0..9 and letters A..F;

total of 16 symbols representing numbers 0-15 with a single symbol each.

For example, `0061` is the scalar value for `a`,

and `1F600` is the scalar value for the grinning face emoji.

Unicode documentation often writes those same numbers as U+0061 and U+1F600.

Crucially, there are no UStr literals. Use the method `.u` on Str to obtain Unicode strings.

`` `foo`.u `` converts the `foo` Str into the equivalent `UStr`.

The method `.u` on UStr reads one or more uppercase hexadecimal numbers

separated by single spaces, and appends the corresponding scalar values.

Only digits 0..9 and uppercase letters A..F are allowed. No extra whitespace is accepted. Malformed input throws.

For example: `` `Hello`.u.u`FB03 0066 0066 0069`+(`World`.u) `` would create a string that may be visualized as "HelloffiffiWorld",

but where the first `ffi` is represented as a single scalar value representing a ligature, while the second `ffi` is represented as three distinct scalar values.

And `` myUString.u`FB03 0066 0066 0069`+(`Hello`.u) `` would append "ffiffiHello" to a pre-existing Unicode string.

Since writing `` `Hello`.u.u`...` `` is clearly redundant, Str also supports `.u` with one argument, that first converts to unicode and then append the new sequence.

This type does support concatenation (+,|,^) as for Str, but this works by simply apppending scalar values one after the other in the sequence.

This may not be desirable. Consider ligatures (where scalar values could/should/may be merged)

and grapheme clusters, where simply sequencing scalar values may accidentally create new undesired grapheme clusters.

More complex methods, like .startsWith(prefix) and .replace(target,dest) are not offered exactly for those reasons.

UStr does not implement DataType: how to turn a UStr in a Str or in an Info is really unclear.

How to compare two Unicode strings is also a highly debated topic.

UStr does offer .equalRepr(other) and .hashRepr so that a user can make an efficient OrderHash[UStr] while reconsidering their life decisions.

Extends: Sealed, ToUStr, ToImm[UStr], JoinStr[UStr]

Methods

+(read UStr):UStr

string concatenation; throws on result size > maxInt

.equalRepr(UStr):Bool

checks if the two unicode strings have the same exact scalar value representation.

.escape:Str

returns a string representing valid Fearless code which yields a string with the same representation of this string when evaluated (will use .u)

.flow:mut Flow[Nat]

return a flow of scalar values.

.hashRepr:Nat

hashes all the scalar values together.

read .imm:UStr

From: ToImm[UStr].imm.

.isEmpty:Bool

true iff size == 0

.isStr:Bool

true if this UStr only uses scalar values allowed inside instances of type Str.

.joinStr(mut Flow[UStr]):UStr

From: JoinStr[UStr].joinStr.

.norm:Norm[UStr]

No documentation yet.

.scalarHex:Str

uppercase hexadecimal Unicode scalar values separated by single spaces.

.size:Nat

the amount of unicode scalar values in this string

.strExact:Opt[Str]

.some containg this string if .isStr; .empty otherwise

.u(Str):UStr

concatenate those scalar values to the end of this string. Example parameter could be `FB03 0066 0066 0069`; throws on malformed input.

read .uStr:UStr

From: ToUStr.uStr.

^:UStr

appends a double quote

^(read UStr):UStr

like + but inserts a double quote between the two strings

|:UStr

appends a newline

|(read UStr):UStr

like + but inserts a newline between the two strings

UStrTest

No documentation yet.

Extends: F[Tests,Tests]

Methods

read #(Tests):Tests

No documentation yet.

From: F[Tests,Tests]#.

UnitTestLog

Log used internally by the UnitTest library.

The log is written to .out/logs/_base/unit_test_log.log.

This log is raw: each call writes exactly the string

representation of s, without adding a timestamp and without adding a

newline. The UnitTest library is responsible for producing the whole

output format.

The live file is intended to be consumed by test tooling while tests are

running. The exact format is part of the UnitTest library/runtime

contract, not part of this general logging API.

The log is still managed by the same circular-log mechanism as Log:

old non-empty logs are moved to stamped archive files, empty logs are

deleted, and only a bounded number of archived logs is kept.

See also: base.Log

Methods

.log(read ToStr):Void

No documentation yet.

UnitTests

Unit tests

This design blends test suites, tests and individual checks.

Basically, a test suite is a Test that only calls `.test` on named Tests,

While a Test is a named Test that only calls `.check`.

We can then arbitrary make trees of test suites or by nesting them in other test suites.

A name name is only reported when the name is present.

Example code with two test suites.

```

MyTests: UnitTests {::

.test TestCars

.test TestPerson

}

TestCars: Test {::

.let x=... //can declare locals

//Test `TestCarsCanMove` inside test suite TestCars

.test TestCarsCanMove: Test {:: .check {..}}

//disabled test with name inside test suite TestCars

.disableTest TestCarsCanFly: Test {:: .check {..}}

//unnamed check in the test suite.. possible but not advised

.check {Cars#(...).assertEquals(MakeCar#23) }

//A single test can have many checks

.test TestCarsCanPark: Test {t-> Block#

.if {..} .do{ t.check {..} }

.if {..} .do{ t.check {..} }

.done}

}

```

Example using logs:

```

Events:InMemoryLog[Event]{"Events"}

...

.checkWithSys { sys -> Block#

.let log=Events.reader(sys);

.do { log.clear }

.do { actionUnderTest# }

.do { log.consume.assertEq({::},Lists#(

Event1#(...),

Event2#(...),

))}

.done

}

```

Example checking that an action fails:

```

.checkWithSys { ::.try#{SomethingThatShouldFail#}.assertInfo}

```

Example checking that an action succeeds:

```

.check{SomethingThatShouldSucceed#}

```
